title: DIY Floating Loft Bed for Small Rooms
summary: A legless loft bed that hangs entirely from wall studs on joist hangers — sized here for a twin mattress, with every fastener load-path landing on real framing, not drywall.
description:
This bed has no legs and no floor footprint — it's a wall cleat, a row of joist-hung studs, and a decking surface, built exactly like a small floor inside a room rather than a piece of furniture. That makes it the right answer for a genuinely tiny room where floor space under the bed matters more than anything else, and the wrong answer anywhere the framing can't support it.

**This build only works if every fastener lands in real wall framing.** Find and mark every stud across the bed's width with a stud finder before cutting a single board, and hang the wall cleats to as many of those studs as you possibly can, two structural screws per stud. This is sized here for a twin mattress (39" wide, 75" long); the wall cleats and studs are cut to that width and length, but the same construction scales to any size as long as you re-measure your own wall's stud layout first.

Joist hangers carry the cross studs from the wall cleats — the same hardware and technique used to frame an actual floor joist, which is exactly the load path this bed needs. For extra security on the back edge, the rear-most stud is also screwed directly into the wall studs behind it, not just hung in its hanger. The 1x6 decking then lays across the studs and gets nailed and glued down, and a 1x6 front trim board caps the open edge — left slightly proud on top so loose items don't roll off, but not a substitute for a real guardrail, which is genuinely recommended here rather than optional.

tools.note: The wall cleats must be level, or the whole deck slopes.

materials.name: Structural screws, 4", star drive
materials.note: Long enough to reach solidly into wall studs.

materials.name: Joist hanger nails or structural screws

cutList.note: Bed width (39") minus 3/4". Re-measure for your own bed width.
Bed length (75") minus 3". Spaced evenly, about every 16-18".
Cut to match your wall cleat length.
Cut to fit the actual front of the assembled bed.

steps.title: Locate studs and hang the wall cleats
steps.body: Find and mark every wall stud across the bed's width with a stud finder before cutting anything. Hang the two 2x4 wall cleats level and secure to as many studs as possible, two structural screws per stud — this is the connection the entire bed's weight travels through.

steps.title: Hang the cross studs
steps.body: Attach a joist hanger to each end of every cross stud, then hang the studs between the two wall cleats. For added support, also screw the back-most stud directly into the wall studs behind it, not just through its hanger.
materials: Joist hanger nails or structural screws

steps.title: Lay the decking
steps.body: Lay the 1x6 decking boards across the framing and nail down with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue.

steps.title: Trim the front and add a guardrail
steps.body: Trim out the front edge with a 1x6 board, cut to fit the actual assembled bed, leaving a slight lip on top to keep items from rolling off. A guardrail along the open edge is strongly recommended to prevent falls, especially for a bed this high off the floor.

images.alt: Wall-mounted floating loft bed with no legs, hung on joist hangers
